era_context: The early 12th century: the Crusader States are consolidating in the Levant following the First Crusade (1096–1099); the Song Dynasty rules southern China while the Jin Dynasty (Jurchen) is rising in the north; the Norman kingdoms are expanding in England and Sicily; and Western Christendom is experiencing the final stages of the Investiture Controversy.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written sources)

events.title: Establishment of the Jin Dynasty in Manchuria
events.description: The Jurchen people, under the leadership of Aguda (later known as Emperor Taizu of Jin), formally established the Jin Dynasty in 1115. The Jin Dynasty would become one of the major powers in East Asia, eventually conquering northern China and establishing the Jin-Song border. The establishment of the Jin Dynasty marked a significant shift in the balance of power in East Asia and would have profound consequences for the Song Dynasty and the entire region. The Jin Dynasty would rule northern China from 1127 to 1234.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Jin Dynasty in 1115 is well-documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is securely established in historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Led to 1127-001 (Jin conquest of northern China)

events.title: Crusader military campaigns in the Levant; expansion of the Kingdom of Jerusalem
events.description: King Baldwin I of Jerusalem continued military campaigns to expand and consolidate Crusader territory in the Levant. In 1115, Baldwin I was actively engaged in campaigns against Muslim forces, particularly in the region of Transjordan and the Dead Sea. These campaigns were part of the broader process of Crusader state-building and territorial expansion. The Crusaders were gradually establishing control over the Palestinian coast and interior regions.
key_figures: King Baldwin I of Jerusalem (Crusader leader)
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Crusader military expansion is well-established, but specific campaigns in 1115 are less precisely dated in available sources.

events.title: Pope Paschal II continues papacy; ongoing Investiture Controversy
events.description: Pope Paschal II (r. 1099–1118) was in the middle years of his papacy in 1115. His reign had been marked by intense conflict with Emperor Henry V over the Investiture Controversy. In 1111, Paschal had negotiated the controversial Concordat of 1111 with Henry V, which temporarily ceded significant power to the emperor in exchange for recognition of papal authority. By 1115, the terms of this concordat were being challenged by reformist elements within the Church, and tensions between the papacy and the empire remained high.
events.certainty_note: Paschal II's papacy and the ongoing Investiture Controversy are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Song Dynasty court politics and military challenges
events.description: Emperor Huizong of Song (r. 1100–1125) was ruling the Song Dynasty in 1115. His reign was marked by cultural patronage and artistic pursuits, but also by military challenges from the rising Jin Dynasty in the north. The Song Dynasty was beginning to face increasing pressure from the Jin Dynasty, which had just been established in 1115. This military pressure would eventually lead to the loss of northern China to the Jin Dynasty in 1127.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Huizong's reign and the broader context of Song-Jin conflict are well-documented in Chinese sources. The specific events of 1115 are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Followed by 1127-001 (Jin conquest of northern China)

events.title: Norman consolidation in Sicily and southern Italy
events.region: Kingdom of Sicily and southern Italy
events.description: The Norman kingdom in Sicily and southern Italy continued to consolidate its power in 1115. The kingdom was ruled by a regency for the young Roger II, who would eventually become one of the most powerful rulers in the Mediterranean. The Normans were actively engaged in establishing their authority over the diverse populations of Sicily and southern Italy, including Greeks, Arabs, and Italians.

sources.name: Falcandus, Liber de Regno Sicilie (Book of the Kingdom of Sicily)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Norman consolidation in Sicily is well-established. The specific events of 1115 are less precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The establishment of the Jin Dynasty in 1115 is sometimes portrayed as a sudden and unexpected event, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Jurchen had been gradually consolidating power in Manchuria for several decades before 1115. The formal establishment of the Jin Dynasty was the culmination of a long process of state-building and military organization. Additionally, the immediate impact of the Jin Dynasty's establishment on the Song Dynasty was not as dramatic as some accounts suggest; the major military conflicts between the Jin and Song did not occur until the 1120s.
historiographic_note: The year 1115 is moderately well-documented in Chinese sources, particularly regarding the establishment of the Jin Dynasty and the Song Dynasty's political situation. Western European sources provide accounts of Crusader military activities and papal affairs. Islamic sources provide parallel accounts of Crusader activities in the Levant. Northern European sources are sparse and often compiled centuries after the events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia have virtually no written sources for this year.

graph_edges.note: The establishment of the Jin Dynasty in 1115 eventually led to the Jin conquest of northern China in 1127.
The Song Dynasty's military challenges in 1115 were directly related to the establishment of the Jin Dynasty.
Crusader military campaigns in 1115 contributed to continued expansion in 1116.
